articles.headline: 10 Sessions of Yoga Cuts Opioid Withdrawal Time Nearly in Half
articles.body: <cite index="51-4">Yogis achieved full withdrawal from opioids more than four times faster with the help of their yoga practice</cite>, according to research published in JAMA Psychiatry. <cite index="52-5,52-6">Fifty-nine men with opioid use disorder were randomly assigned to yoga or control groups. The yoga intervention included 10 supervised 45-minute sessions for 14 days alongside standard buprenorphine treatment, while control participants received buprenorphine treatment alone.</cite> <cite index="50-4">The intervention appeared to help with anxiety symptoms, improved sleep, curbed pain, and ultimately helped restore balance to the autonomic nervous system.</cite> The study offers concrete evidence for an inexpensive tool that could improve recovery outcomes.
articles.takeaway: Yoga as an add-on to standard treatment dramatically speeds opioid withdrawal recovery and improves autonomic regulation—worth integrating into any recovery protocol.

articles.topic: Sprint training preserves cycling fitness off-season
articles.headline: Adding Sprints to Easy Rides Keeps You Sharp During Off-Season
articles.body: <cite index="41-1">Including sprints in one weekly low-intensity session during the transition period gave elite cyclists a performance advantage six weeks into the subsequent preparatory period.</cite> <cite index="41-7,41-8">A control group performed low-intensity training only, while a sprint group added three sets of three 30-second maximal sprints to one weekly low-intensity session. Performance and physiological measures were compared between the end of the competitive season and six weeks into the preparatory period.</cite> <cite index="41-10">This gain coincided with higher oxygen uptake during exercise, suggesting the strategy may help preserve training stimulus despite reduced overall training load.</cite> Perfect for maintaining fitness without total volume.
articles.takeaway: During your off-season, add brief maximal sprints to one easy session per week. No extra training volume needed, but your fitness preservation improves measurably.

product.name: Garmin Forerunner 255
product.blurb: The watch Chris trains with daily. Tracks every discipline without breaking the bank.

articles.topic: AI wearables detect illness before symptoms appear
articles.headline: Your Ring Is Now Smarter Than You About Getting Sick
articles.body: <cite index="63-1,63-2">Oura's illness detection feature, which monitors overnight temperature and resting heart rate, has been shown to detect viral illness 1-3 days before symptoms appear in many users. This is a genuinely useful capability that raw data alone cannot provide without statistical modeling.</cite> <cite index="59-2,59-4">Advanced AI algorithms are dramatically improving the accuracy of wearable health sensors. Wearable technology in 2026 has evolved into a continuous, AI-powered health intelligence system—capable of predicting disease, optimizing performance, and even augmenting human capabilities.</cite> The shift from reactive to proactive monitoring is now real, not hype.
articles.takeaway: Your wearable is learning to predict illness before you feel it. Pay attention to elevated HRV and temperature alerts—they often precede symptoms by days.
articles.pexelsQuery: smart ring wearable technology health monitoring

product.name: Garmin Forerunner 255
product.blurb: The watch Chris trains with daily. Tracks every discipline without breaking the bank.

articles.topic: Positive age beliefs predict cognitive physical gains
articles.headline: Your Belief About Aging Just Rewired Your Brain. For Better.
articles.body: <cite index="75-1,75-3">A decade-long study of over 11,000 older Americans found that nearly half (45%) of adults aged 65 and older actually showed measurable improvement in cognitive or physical function over time.</cite> <cite index="74-15">Researchers found that individuals who held more positive beliefs about ageing were significantly more likely to experience improvements in both cognitive performance and physical function.</cite> <cite index="76-1,76-2">Previous studies led by Levy found that negative beliefs about aging are associated with poorer memory, slower walking speed, increased cardiovascular risk, and biomarkers linked to Alzheimer's disease. According to Levy, the new findings show the opposite pattern can also occur.</cite> Mindset isn't motivational fluff—it's biology.
articles.takeaway: Stop assuming decline. Your beliefs about aging directly shape whether you improve or decline. Positive age beliefs are a stronger predictor of gains than age itself.
articles.pexelsQuery: older adult healthy active strength training

workout.title: Push & Press: Chest, Shoulders, Triceps Circuit
workout.tip: If your lower back starts to arch on any pressing movement, reduce the weight and focus on squeezing your core first. A tight midsection protects your spine and makes every push stronger.
workout.warmup: Start with 60 seconds of arm circles (forward and back), then 10 slow shoulder rolls each side and 10 wall push-ups at an easy tempo. Finish with 30 seconds of light chest openers with arms spread wide.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Chest Press
exercises.notes: Lie on a bench or floor. Lower dumbbells to chest level with elbows at 45 degrees, not flared wide. Drive up to full extension without locking out hard.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Overhead Press (seated or standing)
exercises.notes: Press from shoulder height to overhead, finishing with palms facing forward. Avoid letting your lower back arch by bracing your core before each rep.

exercises.name: Push-Up
exercises.notes: Keep body in a straight line from heels to head. Drop to knees if you need to keep good form. Move at a controlled 2-second down, 1-second up tempo.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Use lighter weight than you think you need. Raise arms to shoulder height only, slight bend in elbow throughout. Pause at the top for one count.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Tricep Kickback
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, upper arm parallel to floor, extend forearm back to straight. Squeeze at the top. Switch arms without resting.

workout.cooldown: Finish with a 60-second chest stretch in a doorway or with hands clasped behind your back. Follow with a gentle overhead tricep stretch, holding 20-30 seconds each side.
workout.description: Warmup: Start with 60 seconds of arm circles (forward and back), then 10 slow shoulder rolls each side and 10 wall push-ups at an easy tempo. Finish with 30 seconds of light chest openers with arms spread wide. Dumbbell Chest Press: 3 sets x 10-12 — Lie on a bench or floor. Lower dumbbells to chest level with elbows at 45 degrees, not flared wide. Drive up to full extension without locking out hard. Dumbbell Overhead Press (seated or standing): 3 sets x 10 — Press from shoulder height to overhead, finishing with palms facing forward. Avoid letting your lower back arch by bracing your core before each rep. Push-Up: 3 sets x 10-15 — Keep body in a straight line from heels to head. Drop to knees if you need to keep good form. Move at a controlled 2-second down, 1-second up tempo. Dumbbell Lateral Raise: 3 sets x 12 — Use lighter weight than you think you need. Raise arms to shoulder height only, slight bend in elbow throughout. Pause at the top for one count. Dumbbell Tricep Kickback: 3 sets x 12 each arm — Hinge at the hips, upper arm parallel to floor, extend forearm back to straight. Squeeze at the top. Switch arms without resting. Cooldown: Finish with a 60-second chest stretch in a doorway or with hands clasped behind your back. Follow with a gentle overhead tricep stretch, holding 20-30 seconds each side.

meal.title: Italian Whole Grain Polenta Bowl with Poached Eggs & Roasted Tomatoes
meal.description: Creamy, buttery polenta made with whole grain cornmeal provides steady carbohydrate fuel, topped with protein-rich poached eggs and sweet roasted cherry tomatoes for a savory Italian-inspired breakfast. Perfect for endurance athletes who want something filling and digestion-friendly before long training days.
ingredients: salt and black pepper to taste
meal.instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 425°F. Toss cherry tomatoes with olive oil, balsamic vinegar, salt, and pepper on a baking sheet. Roast for 15 minutes until blistered.
2. Meanwhile, bring vegetable broth to a gentle boil in a small pot. Slowly whisk in polenta, stirring constantly to avoid lumps.
3. Reduce heat to low and continue stirring for 15–20 minutes until polenta is creamy and pulls away slightly from the pot sides. Stir in butter and Parmesan; season with salt and pepper.
4. Fill a shallow skillet with water, add white vinegar, and bring to a gentle simmer. Create a gentle whirlpool with a spoon, then slide eggs into the center one at a time. Poach 3–4 minutes until whites are set but yolks remain runny.
5. Pour polenta into a bowl, top with roasted tomatoes and poached eggs, and garnish with fresh basil.
meal.tip: Polenta can be made up to 2 days ahead and stored in the fridge; reheat gently with a splash of broth over low heat while you roast fresh tomatoes and poach eggs on race morning. The hot polenta won't spike your blood sugar as hard as simple carbs, making it ideal for sustained endurance.
